questions.text: The tendency of undisturbed objects to stay at rest or keep moving with the same velocity is called:
questions.explanation: Inertia is the natural resistance of an object to any change in its state of motion.

questions.text: Newton's second law of motion gives us a measure of:
questions.explanation: F = ma. The second law quantifies force as the product of mass and acceleration.

questions.text: The momentum of an object of mass 'm' moving with a velocity 'v' is:
questions.explanation: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv).

questions.text: When a passenger in a moving bus leans forward when the bus brakes suddenly, it is due to:
questions.explanation: The upper body continues to move forward due to inertia while the bus stops.

questions.text: A fielder pulls his hands backwards while catching a cricket ball to:
questions.explanation: By increasing the time of catch, the rate of change of momentum (force) is reduced.

questions.text: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This is Newton's:
questions.explanation: The third law describes how forces always exist in action-reaction pairs.

questions.text: If the mass of an object is doubled while the force remains constant, the acceleration will be:
questions.explanation: Since a = F/m, doubling mass results in half the acceleration.

questions.text: The SI unit of force is:
questions.explanation: One Newton is defined as 1 kg·m/s².

questions.text: In a collision between a small car and a heavy truck, which experiences a greater force?
questions.explanation: According to Newton's Third Law, the action and reaction forces are equal and opposite.

questions.text: The total momentum of an isolated system remains constant. This is known as:
questions.explanation: In the absence of external force, total momentum before and after impact is the same.

questions.text: The measure of inertia of an object is its:
questions.explanation: Mass is a quantitative measure of inertia; heavier objects are harder to move or stop.

questions.text: Newton's First Law is also known as the:
questions.explanation: It states that an object resists changes to its state of motion (inertia).

questions.text: When we shake the branch of a tree, some leaves get detached due to:
questions.explanation: The branch moves while the leaves tend to stay at rest due to inertia.

questions.text: Force is required to change the ________ of an object.
questions.explanation: Force is the external agency that changes velocity (speed or direction) or state of rest.

questions.text: A bullet of mass 10g is fired from a gun of mass 2kg with velocity 100 m/s. The recoil velocity of the gun is:
The product of force and time for which it acts is called:
questions.explanation: Impulse (F * t) is equal to the change in momentum of the object.

questions.text: Balanced forces acting on a body ________.
options: Cannot change its state of rest
questions.explanation: Balanced forces have zero net force, so they don't change motion but can deform objects.

questions.text: Why do athletes jump on a sand bed or cushion?
options: To reduce force by increasing time
questions.explanation: Sand bed increases the time taken to stop, reducing the impact force (Rate of momentum change).

questions.text: Rocket propulsion is based on the principle of ________.
questions.explanation: Backward momentum of gases equals forward momentum of the rocket.

questions.text: The SI unit of momentum is:
questions.explanation: Momentum = mass (kg) * velocity (m/s).

questions.text: When a carpet is beaten with a stick, dust comes out due to:
questions.explanation: Carpet moves suddenly while dust particles tend to stay at rest.

questions.text: Action and reaction forces ________.
questions.explanation: Action and reaction always act on two different interacting objects.

questions.text: If no external force acts on a system of bodies, their total momentum ________.
questions.explanation: This is the statement of the Law of Conservation of Momentum.

questions.text: A force of 5N gives a mass m1 an acceleration of 10 m/s². The mass m1 is:
Friction is a ________ force.
questions.explanation: Friction arises due to the contact between two surfaces.

questions.text: Why is it difficult to walk on a oily or slippery floor?
questions.explanation: Reduced friction makes it hard to get the necessary reaction force to push forward.

questions.text: The rate of change of momentum is directly proportional to ________.
questions.explanation: This is the mathematical statement of Newton's Second Law.

questions.text: A passenger in a moving train tosses a coin. The coin falls behind him. The train is moving with:
questions.explanation: The train speeds up while the coin maintains the previous horizontal speed.

questions.text: Swimming is possible due to Newton's ________.
questions.explanation: Swimmer pushes water back (action) and water pushes swimmer forward (reaction).

questions.text: The inertia of a body depends on its ________.
questions.explanation: Mass is the fundamental property that determines inertia.

questions.text: Which law of motion is used to define the unit of force?
questions.explanation: Second Law (F=ma) is used to define 1 Newton (1 kg m/s²).

questions.text: A body of mass 2kg is moving with velocity 5 m/s. Its momentum is:
If a net force acts on an object, the object will ________.
questions.explanation: An unbalanced force causes a change in velocity (acceleration).

questions.text: Internal forces in a system ________ change the total momentum.
questions.explanation: Only external forces can change the total momentum of a system.

questions.text: Force is a ________ quantity.
questions.explanation: Force has both magnitude and direction.

questions.text: Why does a passenger lean outwards when a bus takes a sharp turn?
questions.explanation: The body tends to maintain its original direction of motion.

questions.text: If you push a wall with 10N force, the wall pushes you with:
questions.explanation: Action and reaction are always equal in magnitude.

questions.text: Which has more inertia: a bicycle or a train?
questions.explanation: A train has much more mass, therefore much more inertia.

questions.text: Newton's Second Law relates force, mass, and ________.
questions.explanation: The formula F = ma connects these three quantities.

questions.text: Momentum of a body at rest is:
questions.explanation: Since v = 0, p = m * 0 = 0.
